en.extract:
The red-faced crimsonwing (Cryptospiza reichenovii) is a common species of estrildid finch found in Africa. It has an estimated global extent of occurrence of 390,000 km2.
It is native to subsaharan mountainous regions : Bioko and the Western High Plateau, the Albertine Rift montane forests and east Africa (from northern Tanzania to eastern Zimbabwe).


Taxonomy
The red-faced crimsonwing was formally described in 1874 by the German ornithologist Gustav Hartlaub based on a female specimen that had been collected by Anton Reichenow near Bondongo (Bonjongo) in west Cameroon, West Africa. Hartlaub coined the binomial name Pytelia reichenovii where the specific epithet was chosen to honour the collector. The red-faced crimsonwing is now one of four species placed in the genus Cryptospiza that was introduced in 1884 by the Italian zoologist Tommaso Salvadori.
Three subspecies are recognised:

C. r. reichenovii (Hartlaub, 1874) – southeast Nigeria, Cameroon, northwest Angola and the island of Bioko
C. r. ocularis Sharpe, 1902 – east DR Congo, southwest Uganda, Rwanda and Burundi
C. r. australis Shelley, 1896 – Tanzania to Malawi, east Zimbabwe and west Mozambique


References


External links
BirdLife International species factsheet
Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds

The '''red-faced crimsonwing''' ('''''Cryptospiza reichenovii''''') is a common species of [[estrildid finch]] found in Africa. It has an estimated global extent of occurrence of 390,000 km<sup>2</sup>.

It is native to subsaharan mountainous regions : [[Bioko]] and the [[Western High Plateau]], the [[Albertine Rift montane forests]] and east Africa (from northern Tanzania to eastern Zimbabwe).

==Taxonomy==
The red-faced crimsonwing was [[species description|formally described]] in 1874 by the German ornithologist [[Gustav Hartlaub]] based on a female specimen that had been collected by [[Anton Reichenow]] near Bondongo ([[Bonjongo]]) in west [[Cameroon]], West Africa. Hartlaub coined the [[binomial name]] ''Pytelia reichenovii'' where the specific epithet was chosen to honour the collector.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Hartlaub | first=Gustav | author-link=Gustav Hartlaub | date=1874 | title=Description of a new species of ''Pytelia'' | journal=Ibis | series=3rd series | volume=4 | pages=166-167 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/56942488 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1968 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=14 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=317 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14481518 }}</ref> The red-faced crimsonwing is now one of four species placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Cryptospiza]]'' that was introduced in 1884 by the Italian zoologist [[Tommaso Salvadori]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Waxbills, parrotfinches, munias, whydahs, Olive Warbler, accentors, pipits | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/waxbills/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=7 August 2025 }}</ref>

Three [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''C. r. reichenovii'' ([[Gustav Hartlaub|Hartlaub]], 1874) – southeast Nigeria, Cameroon, northwest Angola and the island of [[Bioko]]
* ''C. r. ocularis'' [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe|Sharpe]], 1902 – east DR Congo, southwest Uganda, Rwanda and Burundi
* ''C. r. australis'' [[George Ernest Shelley|Shelley]], 1896 – Tanzania to Malawi, east Zimbabwe and west Mozambique

zh.extract:
红脸朱翅雀（学名：Cryptospiza reichenovii）是非洲的一种常见梅花雀。其全球分布范围约为390,000平方千米。
红脸朱翅雀分布于安哥拉、布隆迪、喀麦隆、刚果民主共和国、马拉维、莫桑比克、尼日尔、尼日利亚、卢旺达、坦桑尼亚、乌干达、赞比亚和津巴布韦。


参考文献
BirdLife International. Cryptospiza reichenovii. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2009.   [2010-06-21] （英语）.
